evidence.title: Acute Otitis Externa - Clinical Practice Guideline | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Topical preparations should be prescribed for initial therapy for diffuse, uncomplicated AOE and delivery enhanced through patient instruction on administration or by performing aural toilet, wick placement, or both, when the ear canal is obstructed.

evidence.title: Acute Otitis Media | Right Decisions
evidence.snippet: 5 days Antimicrobial recommendation Amoxicillin 500mg every 8 hours orally Penicillin Allergy Doxycycline 200mg stat then 100mg daily orally Notes Seek advice from ENT if:

evidence.title: Acute Otitis Externa: Rapid Evidence Review | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Medication Dosing Cost * Use if tympanic membrane perforated Comments Acetic acid 2% Four to six times daily $15 for 15 mL (—) No May be less effective; can use for prophylaxis Ciprofloxacin 0.2%/hydrocortisone 1% (Cipro HC) Twice daily — for 10 mL ($330) No Less risk for sensitization Ciprofloxacin 0.3%/dexamethasone 0.1% (Ciprodex Otic) Twice daily $50 for 7.5 mL ($250) Yes Less risk for sensitization Hydrocortisone 1%/acetic acid 2% Four to six times daily $15 for 10 mL (—) No May cause irritation and pain Neomycin/polymyxin B/hydrocortisone (Cortisporin) Three to four times daily $25 for 10 mL (—) No May cause hypersensitivity or contact reactions Ofloxacin 0.3% One to two times daily $15 for 5 mL, $25 for 10 mL (—) Yes Less risk for sensitization

note_text:
S: The patient is a 51-year-old female presenting with a 3-day history of a severe left earache, described as a stopped-up sensation. She denies fever currently but recalls chills around the onset of symptoms. There is no ear drainage, hearing loss, or ringing in the ears. She denies pain or swelling around the jaw, neck, or head, as well as dizziness or balance problems. She has no recent history of allergies, sinus infections, or nasal congestion but reports swimming about a week ago. She has taken acetaminophen (Tylenol) without relief.

O: Phone-only encounter; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is acute otitis externa, likely related to recent water exposure from swimming and presenting with ear pain and a sensation of ear fullness without drainage or systemic symptoms.
Differential:
1. Acute otitis externa
2. Acute otitis media
3. Eustachian tube dysfunction
4. Cerumen impaction

P: - First-line medication: Ciprofloxacin 0.3% otic drops, 4 drops into the affected ear twice daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Alternatives: Ofloxacin 0.3% otic drops, 10 drops into the affected ear once daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
- Medication safety considerations: No allergies reported; not pregnant; no renal or liver disease; monitor for local irritation or hypersensitivity
- Follow-up recommendations: Seek in-person medical care urgently if ear pain worsens, fever develops, ear drainage appears, or hearing loss occurs; seek emergency care if severe systemic symptoms develop
- Testing: Not indicated at this time based on telephone assessment
